timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Call of Jeremiah

sections.header: Verses 1–10 — "You Have Lived as a Prostitute"

heb.paragraph: The verb šûb is the central wordplay of chapter 3, appearing in multiple forms. It means both "to return" (geographically or spiritually) and "to repent." God asks Israel to šûb (return) but notes she has only done so bĕšeqer (in falsehood, v.10). Judah's "repentance" under Josiah's reforms was superficial — outward compliance without inward transformation.

The noun mĕšubâ derives from the same root šûb but with a negative prefix — it means "turning away" rather than "turning back." Israel is called mĕšubâ (faithless/backsliding), a pun on the very verb of repentance. She has turned, but in the wrong direction.

panels.ctx: Chapter 3 develops the marriage metaphor introduced in chapter 2. Deuteronomy 24:1–4 prohibited a divorced woman from returning to her first husband after marrying another — yet God invites Israel to return despite her serial unfaithfulness. This exceeds the legal provision and reveals grace beyond law. The comparison between the northern kingdom (Israel, exiled in 722 BC) and Judah is damning: Judah saw what happened to Israel and still did not learn. Judah is worse than Israel because she sinned with the warning of precedent.

cross.note: The legal background: a divorced woman cannot return to her first husband. God transcends his own law by inviting faithless Israel back — grace overrides legal provision.
Hosea's marriage to Gomer provides the fullest prophetic development of the marriage metaphor — God as the faithful husband pursuing an unfaithful wife.
Jesus explains that Mosaic divorce law reflected hardness of heart, not God's ideal — the same tension Jeremiah navigates between legal stricture and divine mercy.

notes.note: The rhetorical question draws on Deuteronomy 24:1–4: if human law forbids a woman to return after remarriage, how much more should Israel expect rejection? Yet the "but" that follows signals divine mercy beyond legal precedent.

The comparison between Israel and Judah is devastating. Faithless Israel (the northern kingdom) committed apostasy and was exiled. Treacherous Judah saw this and "did not return to me with all her heart, but only in pretense" — Josiah's reforms were external, not internal.

God "gave faithless Israel her certificate of divorce" — the fall of Samaria in 722 BC. Yet Judah was not warned. The historical precedent that should have produced repentance produced only complacency.

Calvin marvels at the tension: God invokes a law that would condemn Israel, then suspends it by grace. The point is not to abolish the law but to show that God's mercy exceeds his own legal framework. What human courts cannot permit, divine love makes possible.

Calvin is scathing about "repentance in pretense" — he calls it the most dangerous form of self-deception, worse than open rebellion. Those who perform repentance without experiencing it inoculate themselves against genuine transformation.

The Hebrew uses a conditional construction: "If a man divorces his wife..." The legal citation creates a framework that God then subverts by extending an invitation that the law itself would prohibit.

The phrase "in the days of King Josiah" places this oracle during the reform period (622–609 BC), suggesting that even during Josiah's revival, Jeremiah recognized the superficiality of the national repentance.

Lundbom identifies this as a disputatio — a rhetorical argument that traps the audience. They must agree with the legal principle (v.1a), which then convicts them of their own situation (v.1b). The form is forensic: the accused condemn themselves by their own standards.

Brueggemann reads the Israel/Judah comparison as a critique of institutional religion. Judah had the temple, the reforms, the outward signs of faithfulness — and it was all "pretense" (šeqer). The most dangerous form of apostasy is the one that looks like orthodoxy.

heb.gloss: I will heal / I will not be angry forever
heb.paragraph: God promises to "heal your faithlessness" (v.22) — using the verb rāpāʾ for spiritual restoration. The metaphor treats apostasy as a disease rather than merely a crime, opening the possibility of cure rather than only punishment.

panels.ctx: Verses 11–18 shift dramatically from indictment to invitation. God calls the northern exiles to return, promising shepherds "after my own heart" (v.15) and a future when the Ark of the Covenant will no longer be needed because God's presence will fill Jerusalem itself (v.16). This is one of the most eschatological passages in early Jeremiah — pointing beyond the present crisis to a transformed future.

cross.note: The promise of faithful shepherds recurs later in Jeremiah, ultimately pointing to the messianic king — the righteous Branch from David's line.
John's vision of the New Jerusalem "without a temple" fulfils Jeremiah's prophecy of a city where the Ark is no longer needed — God's unmediated presence replaces all cultic apparatus.

notes.note: "I will give you shepherds after my own heart" — a messianic promise that looks beyond the present corrupt leadership to future rulers who genuinely know God. The plural "shepherds" suggests a succession culminating in the ultimate Shepherd.

The Ark of the Covenant will no longer be remembered or remade. This is a stunning statement — the Ark was the supreme symbol of God's presence. Its obsolescence points to a new covenant reality where symbol gives way to substance.

Calvin emphasises the divine initiative in repentance: "Return ... for I am your husband." God does not wait for Israel to return on her own initiative — he calls, and the calling makes the return possible. Repentance is a gift before it is an act.
The phrase "it will not be remade" (lōʾ yēʿāśeh ʿôd) implies the Ark's permanent obsolescence. This was a radical statement that challenged the centrality of Ark theology in Jerusalem's cult.

Lundbom argues this section is an independent oracle inserted into the lawsuit context to provide a counter-voice of hope. Its placement between two indictments (vv.1–10 and vv.19–25) creates a chiastic structure: judgment–hope–judgment, reflecting the book's larger rhythm.

Brueggemann sees this passage as a vision of "world turned right side up" — faithful leaders, divine presence without mediation, and nations gathered to Jerusalem. The prophetic imagination refuses to let the present crisis define the future.

sections.header: Verses 19–25 — "We Come to You, for You Are the LORD Our God"

heb.paragraph: God longed to be called "my Father" (ʾābî) by Israel (v.19). The paternal metaphor complements the spousal one — God is both husband and father, and Israel's infidelity wounds both relationships. The liturgy of repentance in vv.22–25 finally uses this address: "You are the LORD our God."

panels.ctx: The chapter closes with a liturgy of repentance (vv.22b–25) that may reflect an actual worship text. The confession is remarkably specific: "The shameful god has consumed the wealth of our ancestors" (v.24). This suggests that Baal worship was not merely a spiritual failing but an economic one — temple revenues and resources diverted to false cults.

cross.note: Hosea likewise provides a liturgy of return with specific language for the penitent: "Take words with you and return to the LORD."
The prodigal son's confession — "Father, I have sinned" — echoes the same pattern of return and address to the Father that Jeremiah envisions.

notes.note: God expresses paternal longing: "I thought you would call me 'Father.'" The anthropomorphism is striking — the sovereign God expresses something like disappointed hope. This is not divine ignorance but relational grief.

The confessional liturgy acknowledges that the "commotion on the hills" (Baal worship) was fraudulent. True salvation is in the LORD alone. The confession moves from negation (idols are futile) to affirmation (God alone saves).

Calvin notes that God's call "Return!" and the simultaneous promise "I will cure your faithlessness" show that repentance and healing are simultaneous. God does not command what he does not enable. The call is itself the cure.

The Hebrew is difficult: God says "I said, 'How gladly I would set you among my sons and give you a pleasant land.'" The verb šît ("set/place") with bānîm ("sons") suggests adoption language — God wanted to grant Israel full inheritance rights.

Lundbom identifies vv.22b–25 as a "confession of sins" liturgy that the prophet composes for the people — giving them words for genuine repentance. This literary technique (speaking for the audience in the first person) invites participation rather than merely pronouncing judgment.

Brueggemann calls this "prophetic ventriloquism" — Jeremiah speaks the words of repentance that the people should speak but have not yet spoken. The prophet performs the community's future conversion in advance, making it imaginatively available.

themes.note: Covenant and Mercy both rise to 7–8 as the chapter balances indictment with invitation. Worship at 7 reflects concern with both false worship (Baal) and the promise of a transformed cult.

rows.label: vv.6–10
rows.text: Israel vs. Judah: Worse Than Her Sister

lit.note: Chapter 3 pivots on the šûb wordplay — "turn/return/repent." The legal impossibility of return (Deut 24) gives way to divine invitation, creating a tension between law and grace that anticipates the new covenant of chapter 31.

ppl.name: Jeremiah
ppl.text: The phrase "in the days of King Josiah" places this oracle during the reform period (622–609 BC), suggesting that even during Josiah's revival, Jeremiah recognized the superficiality of the national repentance…

ppl.name: Josiah
ppl.text: Treacherous Judah saw this and "did not return to me with all her heart, but only in pretense" — Josiah's reforms were external, not internal…

trans.title: Comparing translations in Faithless Israel, Treacherous Judah

translations.text: “Look up to the barren heights and see. Is there any place where you have not been ravished? By the roadside you sat waiting for lovers, sat like a nomad in the desert. You have defiled the land with your prostitution and wickedness.

Lift up your eyes to the bare heights, and see! Where have you not been ravished? By the waysides you have sat awaiting lovers like an Arab in the wilderness. You have polluted the land with your vile whoredom.
Because Israel’s immorality mattered so little to her, she defiled the land and committed adultery with stone and wood.
Because she took her whoredom lightly, she polluted the land, committing adultery with stone and tree.
